new_risk_factors: Minimum wage increases driving labor cost escalation for franchisees and company stores
Privacy and data protection regulatory evolution potentially impacting marketing strategies
Difficulties obtaining licenses or approvals could delay new store openings
International tariffs and regulations on imported commodities and equipment
FTC and state franchise disclosure/registration compliance obligations
Environmental legislation uncertainty for future periods
Security breach notification requirements under new U.S. and international laws
positive_catalysts_cited: Environmental compliance: no material capital expenditures anticipated in 2026
Belief that franchise disclosure documents comply in all material respects with applicable laws
key_quotes: A significant number of both our and our franchisees' food service personnel are paid at rates related to the applicable minimum wage, and past increases in the minimum wage have increased labor costs, as would future increases

The legislative and regulatory landscape for privacy and data protection continues to evolve, and there has been an increase in attention given to privacy and data protection issues with the potential to directly affect our business
Any changes in privacy or data protection laws or regulations could also impact our marketing techniques and could change our marketing strategies
Global retail sales, excluding foreign currency impact, increased 6.3% as compared to the third quarter of 2024 [REMOVED — same store sales 5.2% U.S., global net store growth 214]
payload.mda_summary: The Q4 delta represents a transition from a Q3 10-Q with concrete operational performance highlights (6.3% global retail sales growth, 5.2% U.S. SSS, 214 net new stores, 12.2% income from operations growth) to boilerplate 10-K regulatory and risk-disclosure language. The newly added language explicitly flags minimum wage as a recurring cost escalation risk for both company and franchisee labor, and introduces heightened privacy/data regulation risk that could constrain marketing flexibility. Analysts may be underweighting the degree to which minimum wage headwinds are being formally re-emphasized as a structural cost pressure entering 2026, alongside the removal of the previously reassuring operational momentum narrative that anchored Q3 expectations.
